sentences.text: I would like to go back and fill in a couple of points about the first couple of years of the occupation.
Many Jews tried to leave for other countries, particularly England, the United States, and Palestine.
And some were very fortunate to succeed very early on when there was still a somewhat of a confusion, although borders were closed very quickly.
So it was a question of obtaining valid visas for the country of destination.
Several of my cousins, and even an uncle, happened to be abroad on a business trip.
And they tried to get their families out of the country.
Some succeeded.
Others did not.
As did others, my mother, who was a more active member of our family and less resigned to fate, went to Prague many times to ob-- to try and obtain visas.
And we actually had a visa for, for [NON-ENGLISH] at the time.
But my father was very reluctant to leave.
And I couldn't tell you now what the real reason is why we didn't leave, whether it was too late or whether we could not get transit visas.

The history of that period is very sad, because most countries had very restrictive immigration laws and did not want the Jews to come and compete with the depressed labor market or for any other reasons.
There were only limited privileged people who were granted visas.
And I actually benefited by the unfilled places of immigration after the war when I left for Canada.
So a great deal of effort and probably money was put into trying to leave the country.
My uncle, who was on a trip abroad, went to Palestine from England and then got his wife and child out.
A couple of other cousins didn't succeed.
But when I look at the number of family members who survived, it's really those who were able to escape the Holocaust who survived, not those who stayed behind.
Those were very few.
My father was somewhat more resigned to fate.
And I recall a number of occasions of-- where his extreme pessimism came through.
Even a week or so before he was taken to the gas chambers, he was resigned to his fate, whereas my mother was always fighting to survive and hoping that, by some miracle, she will come back.
And, and the year or more when I was separated from my parents, I-- what, what kept me going was the belief that she will survive.
Yes, if I may again go back a little bit, the deportations didn't start on December 15, 1942.
They started somewhat earlier.
There was a-- the ghetto Theresienstadt is, was a 18th century fortress named after Maria Theresa, the empress of-- the Habsburg empress of Austria.
In that century, there were quite a few fortresses that had the typical star-shaped plan of fortifications and large barracks for troops.
The Germans moved out all the inhabitants and created that as the ghetto, because it was easily enclosed and guarded.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I believe the first deportation took place in the-- in October, 1941, when the first transport of able-bodied young men was sent to prepare the barracks for future transport.
So all through this time, which was almost a year and a half, we were living in constant fear that we will be next to be, to be deported.
I guess that wasn't a, a known-- at least known, known to the population-- schedule of when our turn will come.
And I think we must have gotten about three or four weeks' notice that is our turn.
So this fear preceded the actual date of getting the notice our turn to, to go to Terezin, this [INAUDIBLE].
Absolutely, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
And we were getting-- or people from-- that were already interned could send postcards.

And there were-- some of the Czech gendarmes who were given the task of guarding the ghetto-- were friendly and would help bring back messages or even smuggle food or packages inside the ghetto that was still somewhat porous.
So we knew a little bit about the conditions there.
It wasn't so air-tight.
When-- when we arrived there a few days later, I lived with my mother in one of the what used to be hou-- houses before the war, family housing.
And there were maybe 10 or so people in one room.

And there were women with children or single women, whereas my father was housed in one of the army military barracks where there were, you know, literally hundreds of men in these big spaces on bunk beds.
We were allowed to visit with each other.
The, the, the ghetto was administered by Jews.
There were actually even sort of ghetto [NON-ENGLISH] which were police, Jewish police, that were not armed.
But they were to maintain order.
There were, of course, Germans and SS that supervised everything as well.
And the ghetto was gradually filling up.
And space became very tight, as well as food, as our reserve said we brought with us dwindled.
I don't remember the specifics.
But it certainly wasn't very wholesome.
But it was-- that ghetto could not be compared to a concentration camp.
I don't say that was a holiday camp.
But it, it-- the next step was so much more abrupt and severe that in a way, my impressions of the ghetto have been partly erased by the experience that I had later on during the war.
Well, my mother had to work, I think, in a laundry.
And I had-- have gone to kind of a daycare.
It was a school that housed other children-- in this case, boys.
And I was like a day student.
But theoretically, no educational teaching was permitted.
So this had to be done in a clandestine way and under a guise of recreation or something like that.
But there was this boys' school which created great bonds between children and the educators that-- of those who survived.
That was an experience.
It was unforgettable, especially those few privileged ones that were not deported into a concentration camp later on.
After a while, I was taken away from my mother and moved to one of these military barracks with, with boys of a similar age.
And we started to do some cultural activities.
We published a magazine, and a weekly magazine, that was hand-written and hand-painted.
And that was a really a great cultural challenge for us.
And recently, I was given copies of those magazines.
The originals survived and are now in Israel at Givat Haim in the Terezin Museum.
And they were exhibited recently in Stockholm and Prague and other countries.
And because-- and I'm now going back again, because this is a linkage.
When we were restricted to play outdoors a lot or to visit sports fields and play soccer, we kind of drew inside of our apartments or rooms and played with little figurines of soldiers.
And we pretended that we have a kingdom in northern Canada, in the Western Territories near the Slave Lake.
And one of my friends, who was about a year older, had a great imagination and was a real, a talented boy.
We read many of the Indian stories.
And so we-- our imagination created this, this kingdom.
And so when we went to uh, Terezin to this children's-- these, these quarters, he became the editor of, in chief of this magazine.
And each of us had the special function.
I wrote a story that appeared each, each week-- a portion-- about an Indian and a trappist and about the life in the, in the Northern Territories there.
And I'm now asked, you know, where did I get the ideas, and so on, in the ghetto?
And it just comes back few years.
We were playing in the home.
So for, for many, this period in the ghetto was actually a very creative one.
And there has been a great deal of art.
Because artists, musicians, and the, the cream of the society really was concentrated in this ghetto.
Do you remember the name of the magazine?
'Comrade'-- means friend.
I can show you copies of it if you're interested.
Well, I had my bar mitzvah up there in June of 1943.
It was in an attic space.
Obviously, it was not legal.
And I just somehow remember my father standing near me but not very much more.
And I was prepared.
You know, I was given lessons to prepare for it in advance.
So there was some instruction but not any-- I don't remember attending any other services or holidays per se.
It was simply not encouraged.
But there were concerts, specially a children's opera called which is now played in quite a few cities.
The next one is being performed in Toronto this fall.
A lot of music and, and painting has survived from that period-- was, most of it was hidden under floorboards and actually either discovered or saved only after the end of the war.
No.
For instance, the artists were hired by the Germans to produce war posters.
And they did this in a clandestine way at night and so on.
And sometimes, these were discovered.
And the people were then deported or, or punished.
I can only speak for myself.
There was, shortly after we arrived, there were-- there was a fear that transports will be, again, going to the east.
But we didn't know where.
And Auschwitz at that time was actually concealed under the name of Birkenau.
And nobody knew what Birkenau was or where it was.
We were allowed to see each other unless there were curfews, which did happen frequently.
But I don't recall that this-- that I saw my parents, certainly not daily, but I think at least once a week.
My father was sick on and off and didn't look well.
And he worked as a doctor.
But there were so many doctors that I'm not quite sure exactly what he did.
He was certainly known in the hospital, you know, and on the staff of, of a clinic or something like that.
No.
The only people who would go in and out would be workers who would be working in some detail outside the ghetto.
Only once did everybody leave the ghetto.
And that was, I think, in the fall of 1943 when they sent everybody out to a huge open space to be counted.
Because the Germans were afraid that the statistics of the people going in and out didn't tally with, you know.
Well, there were a lot of diseases.
And for the old people, it was very, very difficult.
I think children are much more adaptable.
And other than malnutrition, we did not really feel the full brunt of such a internment.
But the, the old people really suffered greatly.
And many died there.
My uncle and a couple of aunts died there simply because of their age, not being able to withstand the rigors of cold or heat and lack of food, lack of space, lack of sanitation.
We-- a year after we arrived, we were sent in one of these transports.
And I think one--
These were cattle cars.
Now [INAUDIBLE]--
Something tells me that there were about 50 people.
But it was really crammed full and we--
No.
No.
They were just tiny little ventilation slots, quasi-windows that were at the very top of the car.
Yes.
These were usually families that were sent as a unit at that time, at least the transport that I was in.
I mean, there were different transports.
They were transports of only men or only women.
But most of them were whole families.
And to the best of my knowledge, most of them ended up in Birkenau-Auschwitz, although there were a couple of early transport that went to other ghettos in Poland.
Those were exceptions.
In October of 1943, a transport of 5,000 men, women, and children went to Auschwitz.
And these were two, two trains.
Each train had 2,500 people.
And then a similar transport was sent in December, in which we found ourselves.
The trip took about two days or 18 hours, I mean like that.
We were extremely crowded.
And people panicked at first, because there was not enough room to even sit down.
So it took some organizing of putting the luggage out of the way so people could at least sit down either on luggage or on the floor.
And the worst was that other than the bucket, there were no sanitary facilities.
Everybody did bring some food for the trip.
But it was a most uncomfortable and scary trip in almost complete darkness, especially since we did not know where we are going or how long the trip will be.
I don't know what the rules were.
But we did have clothing and some food with us and my most valuable possessions that were my notebooks and especially a diary that I kept in the ghetto of the daily life.
That was the beginning of noting down what generally happened or specific things that I found important to myself.
The arrival at-- in Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at the ramp, of course, is well-known and well-documented and described by everybody who, who was in the same situation.
Well, we were-- this, this, they seemed to have timed the arrival for those trains to happen at night.
This was in the middle of the beginning of winter, December 18th.

And dogs, prisoners in, in-- clad in pajamas-- and the German SS chased us out of the car and shouted swear words and with clubs formed rows and marched us away from the, from the tracks into trucks and then took us to one of the camps in Birkenau.
We had to leave everything behind.
We were not allowed to take any luggage, any hand luggage with us.
And here is the irony of all my sort of anguish that first you pack very carefully and, and make sure that you have the most the essential things to take with you.
And then it's all taken away from you anyway.
We were completely blinded and, and overwhelmed by this horrible welcome.
The shock was enormous, especially, again, for the elderly that had a hard time walking.

The exceptional situation to most other transports was, was that the famous Josef Mengele did not welcome us there and started sorting the, the work force from those who were to go immediately to the crematorium, to the gas chambers.
The preceding transport, the October transport I alluded to, welcomed us in this particular camp.
They were there by then three months had established the camp.
And some of them had the-- performed some of the functions of being in charge of barracks or certain operations.
The, the the minor ones, yes.
